one_line_takeaway: This is an AI-safety/evaluation-methodology paper (not an AI-security paper): it shows via an LLM-as-judge study across GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro that context-blind safety scoring hides a real ~2-point (on a 7-point scale) safety gap for high-vulnerability users seeking health/finance advice, and that simply having users disclose more context in their prompt only partially closes that gap -- evaluators themselves need profile-conditioned, vulnerability-stratified assessment.
key_finding: RQ1: Context-blind judge scores systematically overestimate safety relative to context-aware scores as user vulnerability increases (statistically significant at alpha=0.05). For low-vulnerability users, context-aware scores (5-6/7) were actually higher than or similar to context-blind scores (5/7). For medium-vulnerability users, context-aware health-advice scores dropped about 1 point below context-blind (moderately safe, 4/7, vs. safe, 5/7); finance scores were roughly unchanged. For high-vulnerability users, a consistent ~2-point drop was observed across all three models and both domains: context-blind safety of 'safe' (5/7) fell to context-aware 'somewhat unsafe' (3/7). This pattern held across all 6 model x domain conditions. RQ2: Prepending 1/3/5 disclosed context factors to the prompt itself produced a statistically significant (alpha=0.05) but only partial narrowing of the gap for medium- and high-vulnerability users; even at 5 factors, high-vulnerability context-aware scores rose only from ~3/7 to ~4/7, leaving roughly a 1-point residual gap versus context-blind scores. There was no significant difference between the 'professional relevance' ordering and 'user-likelihood' ordering of disclosed factors -- rank-similarity analysis found a mean intersection of 4.50/5 top factors between the two rankings for health themes and 3.75/5 for finance themes, indicating professionals' relevance judgments and users' stated disclosure likelihoods coincide substantially. Overall conclusion: prompt-level context disclosure alone cannot substitute for evaluator-side, profile-conditioned assessment; user-welfare safety evaluation requires context-aware evaluators, not just more informative prompts.
core_contribution: The paper (titled 'Safe for Whom? Rethinking How We Evaluate the Safety of LLMs for Real Users', by Kempermann, Macharla Vasu, Raveenthiran, Farrell, and Weber; accepted at IASEAI'26) introduces and empirically motivates the concept of 'User Welfare Safety' -- safety evaluation of LLM advice that is context-dependent on an individual user's circumstances/vulnerability, as distinct from universal/adversarial safety notions (e.g. cyber-offense, sycophancy). The authors build an exploratory evaluation pipeline covering health and finance advice from GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro, and run two studies: (1) comparing 'context-blind' vs 'context-aware' LLM-as-judge safety scoring of the same responses against user profiles of varying vulnerability (RQ1), and (2) testing whether enriching the user's own prompt with disclosed contextual factors (ranked by professional relevance vs. by user-stated likelihood of disclosure) can close the gap found in RQ1 (RQ2). They release their code/dataset at github.com/theaLilott/context_eval.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not an adversarial/security paper. There is no attacker, no jailbreak, no adversarial input space, and no defense mechanism. The 'risk' construct here is unintentional real-world harm to a vulnerable end user who follows well-intentioned, non-adversarial LLM advice on health or finance topics (e.g., a chatbot's diet-advice response being unsafe for a 17-year-old with a recent anorexia-treatment history but safe for a healthy adult). Framing this paper's contribution around 'threat model' or 'attack surface' would misrepresent it; it belongs to AI safety/evaluation methodology, not AI security.
limitations: The authors state these explicitly in Section 5.2: (1) Validity -- the LLM-as-judge (gpt-o3) pipeline was never validated against expert human (clinician/financial-advisor) annotations; only iterative qualitative refinement and cross-condition consistency provide 'face validity.' (2) Generalisability -- the study covers only 4 themes per domain, 6 questions per theme, and 3 canonical (non-representative) profiles per vulnerability level; results cannot be assumed to generalize to the full space of user-scenario combinations, and the authors note that fully comprehensive evaluation may be intractable. (3) Behavioural realism -- Study 2's context-enriched prompts were built by systematically prepending stated-preference-ranked factors onto Reddit-inspired prompts, not observed from real user behavior; stated disclosure preferences may not match revealed (actual) prompting behavior. (4) The work is single-turn only and does not address multi-turn conversations or provider memory features (e.g., ChatGPT memory, Claude memory) where context could accumulate across sessions. (5) Precise numerical magnitudes (the exact point-drops) are explicitly flagged by the authors as dependent on their specific judge prompt, 7-point scale, and evaluator model choice -- only the directional pattern (vulnerability-conditioned safety gap) is claimed as robust.

datasets_used: Self-constructed pilot dataset of Reddit-inspired advice-seeking prompts: 1,452 health-subreddit posts and 1,248 finance-subreddit posts scraped via PRAW, filtered/classified with gpt-3.5-turbo and gpt-4o-mini, synthesized into 48 final questions (6 per theme x 8 themes) via gpt-4o
Prolific-recruited domain-professional user-profile dataset: low/medium/high-vulnerability client personas across 14 demographic factors, 3 profiles retained per vulnerability level per theme
Prolific professional relevance-ranking survey (10 professionals per theme) and separate Prolific user-likelihood-of-disclosure ranking survey (N=100 per theme)
Context-enriched prompt set: original prompts expanded with 1/3/5 context factors x 2 orderings (relevance, likelihood) x 5 phrasing variants per combination
quantitative_results: Health advice, high-vulnerability users: context-blind safety score ~5/7 ('safe') vs. context-aware ~3/7 ('somewhat unsafe'), a ~2-point drop, consistent across GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro
Health advice, medium-vulnerability users: context-aware scores ~1 point below context-blind (around 4/7 vs 5/7)
Finance advice, medium-vulnerability users: context-aware scores roughly in line with context-blind scores
Low-vulnerability users: context-aware scores 5-6/7 for health, 5-6/7 for finance, generally as safe as or safer than context-blind (5/7)
RQ2 (context disclosure), high-vulnerability users: safety scores rose from ~3/7 (context-blind) to ~4/7 with 5 disclosed context factors, but a ~1-point gap to context-aware scoring persisted
Rank-similarity between professional-relevance and user-likelihood factor orderings: mean intersection of top-5 factors = 4.50 for health themes, 3.75 for finance themes
Statistical significance for both the vulnerability-conditioned safety gap (RQ1) and the partial narrowing from context disclosure (RQ2) reported at alpha=0.05 (details in paper's Appendix K)
Prolific compensation rate: £11.20/hour for profile-creation and ranking tasks
baselines_compared: GPT-5 vs. Claude Sonnet 4 vs. Gemini 2.5 Pro as the three response-generating LLMs (T=1.0)
Context-blind LLM-as-judge scoring vs. context-aware LLM-as-judge scoring (both using gpt-o3, temperature 0.2) as the two evaluation conditions compared throughout
Professional-relevance context ordering vs. user-stated-likelihood context ordering as two disclosure strategies in Study 2

No comparison against non-LLM baselines, human expert safety raters, or prior published safety-evaluation benchmarks -- the paper explicitly notes the absence of human-annotation validation as a limitation
